data.title: NANO Nuclear Energy slumps after pricing $36M securities offering
data.description:
NANO Nuclear Energy (NASDAQ:NNE) shares dropped over 9% on Thursday after the nuclear energy and technology company announced pricing of an upsized securities offering.

The company priced over 2.11M shares of common stock and warrants, to purchase 1,058,823 shares of common stock, at $17.00 to raise approximately $36M in gross proceeds.

In addition, the company has granted the underwriter a 30-day overallotment option to purchase up to an additional 317,646 shares and/or warrants to purchase 158,823 shares at the public offering price for gross proceeds of up to $5.4M.

The Benchmark Company, LLC is acting as sole book-running manager for the offering.

NANO Nuclear (NNE) intends to use the net proceeds from this offering for research and developments, business development activities and regulatory compliance, intellectual property protection, hiring additional.

The offering is expected to close on or about October 25, 2024, subject to the satisfaction of customary closing conditions.

data.title: Hasbro's profits intact on cost-cutting, but sales guidance sees further erosion
data.description:
Cost-cutting was once again effective in protecting Hasbro’s (NASDAQ:HAS) profits, as net income improved 9 cents per share from a year ago and led the company to maintain its adjusted EBITDA target for FY24 of $975M to $1.025M.

“Outperformance within our gaming and licensing businesses in the third quarter highlights the strength in two of our highest profit areas,” said CEO Chris Cocks. “Our key initiatives around digital, licensing and reinvigorating our product innovation are bearing fruit.”

However, mirroring Mattel’s (MAT) Q3 results on Wednesday, sales remained soft as sluggish consumer demand for toys led to a larger-than-expected 15% drop in total revenue to $1.28B. Excluding the eOne divestiture, sales were down 9%. But with $87M of net cost savings during the quarter, Hasbro’s (HAS) profit improved by a better-than-expected 9 cents to $1.73 per share.

The company’s games division saw revenue drop by 10% while its adjusted operating margin improved to 15.1% on supply chain efficiencies and lowered operating expenses. Sales in the entertainment division plummeted 86%, largely due to the eOne divestiture.

For the remainder of the year, Hasbro (HAS) now expects sales in its Consumer Products segment to be down 12% to 14%, a revision from its prior outlook for a decline of 7% to 11%. The adjusted operating margin outlook for this segment remains unchanged at 4% to 6%.

For Wizards of the Coast, revenue is now expected to be flat to down 1% versus prior guidance of down 1% to down 3% with an unchanged operating margin of ~42%. Entertainment revenue is likely to be down by $15M, also unchanged from previous guidance.

Hasbro (HAS) opened 5% lower Thursday, making today the fourth consecutive day in the red. Mattel (MAT) shares were up more than 4%.

.

data.title: Lilium drops 60% with subsidiaries expected to file for insolvency in Germany
data.description:
Lilium N.V. (NASDAQ:LILM) plunged in early trading on Thursday after the eVTOL company disclosed that it received an indication that the budget committee of the parliament of the Federal Republic of Germany would not approve a €50 million guarantee of a contemplated €100 million convertible loan for Lilium (LILM) from KfW.

The company also said that it has not reached an agreement with the Free State of Bavaria regarding a guarantee of at least €50 million. In addition, Lilium (LILM) said it has not been able to raise sufficient additional funds to continue the operations of Lilium GmbH and Lilium eAircraft GmbH, Lilium’s principal operating wholly owned German subsidiaries. As a consequence, the managing directors of the subsidiaries have determined that they are overindebted and are or will become unable to pay their existing liabilities due within the next few days. The management of the subsidiaries has informed the company that they have to file for insolvency under German law, and in doing so, will apply for self-administration proceedings in Germany.

Looking back: Lilium N.V. (LILM) is a German aerospace company founded in 2015 by four engineers and PhD students from the Technical University of Munich. The company's primary focus is the development of the Lilium Jet, an electric vertical take-off and landing aircraft designed for urban air mobility. The company's journey began with the unmanned flight of its two-seat "Eagle" prototype in April 2017. This was followed by the successful test flight of the five-seat Lilium Jet prototype in May 2019. The company has faced challenges, including a fire that destroyed its first prototype in February 2020. In September 2021, Lilium went public through a SPAC merger and was listed on NASDAQ.

Shares of Lilium (LILM) were down 60% to $0.23 following the development.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: UPS, TSLA, IBM, and more
data.description:
Stock futures trading mixed on Thursday as investors weighed a slew of corporate earnings reports.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Tesla (TSLA) shares surged 11% after the EV leader reported strong Q3 profits. The automotive gross margin excluding credits came in at 17.1%, surpassing the consensus estimate of 15.1%. The operating margin rose to 10.8% of sales, up from 6.3% last quarter and 7.6% last year. CEO Elon Musk announced that Tesla will begin producing more affordable models in the first half of 2025, with vehicle production projected to grow by 20% to 30% in 2025. Cybercab production is anticipated to scale up aggressively in 2026. On autonomy, Musk highlighted continued improvement in miles-per-intervention metrics and anticipates that Full Self-Driving (FSD) will surpass human driving safety by Q2 2025. Additionally, Tesla plans to launch ride-hailing services in Texas and California next year.
  • United Parcel Service (UPS) shares soared 9% after surpassing Q3 earnings expectations, with CEO Carol Tomé highlighting a return to revenue and profit growth after 18 challenging months. Revenue increased 5.4% Y/Y to $22.2 B, driven by growth across all segments. Consolidated operating profit surged 47.8% to $2B, with an adjusted operating margin rate of 8.9%, exceeding the consensus estimate of 8.5%. UPS expects full-year revenue of $91.1B, slightly below the $91.9B forecast, and announced plans for dividend payments of around $4 billion, pending board approval.
  • Seadrill (SDRL) shares surged 12% following reports of ongoing discussions with Transocean (RIG) about a potential merger. The combination would align with Transocean's strategy of expanding its backlog of work, utilizing some of the world's most advanced deepwater drilling rigs. Both companies have faced challenges amid a prolonged downturn in the offshore drilling industry. However, the sector is experiencing renewed growth due to steady oil prices and slower production from U.S. shale fields.
  • Lam Research (LRCX) shares rose nearly 6% after the chip equipment maker reported FQ1 results and guidance that exceeded expectations. The company reported 19.8% Y/Y revenue growth, driven by a 16% increase in systems revenue and a 25% rise in customer support and other revenue. For Q2, Lam Research expects revenue of $4.3Bn with a gross margin of 46.9% plus or minus 1%. Earnings per share are projected to be $0.87 plus or minus 10 cents, surpassing the consensus estimate of $0.85 per share and $4.22B in revenue.

Biggest stock losers

  • International Business Machines (IBM) shares dropped over 4% after the company posted mixed Q3 results, with revenue missing consensus expectations. Software revenue grew by 10%, Consulting remained flat, and Infrastructure revenue declined by 7%. IBM CEO Arvind Krishna expressed confidence in achieving more than $12B in free cash flow for the year, driven by continued expansion of operating margins. For Q4, IBM expects constant currency revenue growth to be consistent with Q3 levels.
  • Boeing (BA) shares fell more than 3% after factory workers voted to reject the company's contract offer, prolonging a six-week strike that has halted production of its top-selling jetliners. The tentative deal, reached over the weekend, proposed a 35% wage increase over four years, along with improvements to benefits and working conditions. The vote coincided with Boeing's report of a $6.2B Q3 loss and a warning that it would remain cash flow negative through 2025 after burning through more than $10B in the first nine months of 2024.
  • WEX (WEX) shares plunged about 13% after the company reported disappointing Q3 results and provided a weak outlook. For Q4 2024, WEX expects revenue between $630M and $640M, below the $673.04M consensus, and adjusted net income of $3.51 to $3.61 per share, missing the $4.26 consensus. For the full year 2024, the company now forecasts revenue between $2.62B and $2.63B, compared to the $2.69B consensus, with adjusted EPS expected to be between $15.21 and $15.31, below the $16.04 consensus.
  • Carrier Global (CARR) lost over 8% following mixed Q3 results. Despite a 21% Y/Y revenue increase, the company missed top-line expectations, though it slightly beat bottom-line estimates. The board also increased its share repurchase authorization to $4.7B. However, Carrier's FY2024 revenue guidance of ~$22.5B fell significantly short of the $25.38B consensus, and its adjusted EPS forecast of $2.50 was well below the $2.85 consensus.

data.title: U.S. Composite PMI inches up in October, driven by service sector
data.description: • S&P Global U.S. Composite PMI (preliminary) edged up to 54.3 in October from 54.0 in September, signaling a slightly faster expansion in business activity to start the last quarter of the year.

data.title: Arca Continental S.A.B. de C.V. reports Q3 results
data.description:

  • Q3 Net Income totaled Ps. 5,132M, up 13.1%, reaching a margin of 8.2%
